data.title: Bill Gates’ daughter lands US$30 million for star-backed AI firm
data.description:
Phoebe Gates, daughter of Microsoft’s billionaire founder Bill Gates and Melinda French Gates, is raising US$30 million in new funding for Phia, a start-up applying artificial intelligence to online shopping, according to a pitch deck seen by Bloomberg.
A spokesperson for the company confirmed the round.
The capital raise will value the New York-based start-up at US$180 million and comes just months after Phia completed its first US$8 million funding round in September.
Phia, which Gates co-founded with Sophia Kianni, is backed by a constellation of celebrities including model and businesswoman Hailey Bieber, Kardashian family matriarch Kris Jenner, former Meta Platforms Chief Operating Officer Sheryl Sandberg, and Spanx founder Sara Blakely.
Bill Gates has not invested, Phoebe Gates told Vogue, but has publicly supported the start-up.
The round will be led by venture capital firm Notable Capital, under managing partner Hans Tung, whose earlier investments include Anthropic and Airbnb. Investor Kleiner Perkins is also participating, alongside Khosla Ventures under partner Keith Rabois.
Phia’s pitch for its AI agent is that online shopping is broken and customers waste time looking for deals or particular items, while brands are targeting the wrong consumers.
The founders are developing an AI-based search engine, offered as an app and browser extension for Chrome desktop and Safari mobile, to help users find items, compare prices and spot deals. The tool has been downloaded 750,000 times in the eight months ending in November, according to the company.
Valuations are ballooning for so-called AI agent start-ups that promise to perform grunt work, saving human workers’ time or replacing them altogether. In its pitch deck, Phia compared itself to fast-growing agent start-ups such as Harvey in the legal sector, recently valued at US$8 billion; Mercor in HR, valued at US$10 billion; and Cursor in software development, valued at US$29.3 billion.
Gates and Kianni began working on the company in their Stanford dorm room, they told Vogue, initially coming up with the idea of a Bluetooth tampon before pivoting to fashion.

data.title: Who was at the Brunello Cucinelli film premiere, from Jonathan Bailey to Italian PM Giorgia Meloni
data.description:
Brunello, Il Visionario Garbato (Brunello: The Gracious Visionary), a film about the life and vision of Italian designer Brunello Cucinelli, premiered in Rome last night.
Known as the King of Cashmere, Cucinelli established his eponymous label in 1978 and has since built a luxury empire based in his hometown of Solomeo, in the Umbria region of Italy.












View this post on Instagram























A post shared by Brunello, Il Visionario Garbato (@brunelloilvisionariogarbato)



A firm believer in what he calls “humanistic capitalism”, Cucinelli has become a sort of philosopher in the luxury industry. He’s always made it a priority to support his native land and Italian craftsmanship, and through philanthropic initiatives that have turned Solomeo into an idyllic hamlet. A-listers ranging from Brad Pitt to Jeff Bezos have visited Cucinelli there.

Directed by Oscar winner Giuseppe Tornatore, the documentary features re-enactments to showcase Cucinelli’s childhood and early days as a striving entrepreneur and designer. Another Oscar winner, Nicola Piovani, composed the soundtrack of the movie, which is out in Italy this month before being released in the US and the rest of the world next year.

In a speech before the screening, Cucinelli referred to Tornatore and Piovani as “poets”. “Poets are the first great people in humankind,” he said. “With this work, these two were able to truly enter my mind. With this film we wanted to combine knowledge, equality and harmony – the qualities [that make] a great human being.”

Celebrities including actors Jessica Chastain, Chris Pine, Jonathan Bailey and Jeff Goldblum, K-pop singer Jinyoung, and Italian Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni attended the premiere, held at Rome’s Cinecittà Studios, where legendary movies such as Cleopatra (1963) and Gangs of New York (2002) were filmed.

After the screening, guests celebrated the designer and his family – including his daughters, Camilla and Carolina Cucinelli – at a glamorous dinner held on a film set recreating Rome’s ancient ruins.

It’s been a busy few days for the designer, whose brand has managed to weather the luxury industry’s current crisis thanks to its focus on timeless luxury that eschews trends. Earlier this week, Cucinelli received the prestigious Outstanding Achievement Award at the Fashion Awards in London.

data.title: 5 of the best things to do in Hong Kong and ways to lend a hand this weekend, December 5-7
data.description:
It remains a challenging time for the Hong Kong community following the devastating fire in Tai Po’s Wang Fuk Court last week.
The outpouring of support as the city mourns the immense loss and witnesses the urgent needs of the displaced reflects the compassion of its people.
This list serves as a dual guide, detailing a few ways you can contribute to the long-term relief and rebuilding efforts for the victims, while also providing activities for families and friends across the city to de-stress and find glimpses of joy over the weekend.
1. The Poet
The Hong Kong food and drink business is rallying together to support families who continue to be deeply affected by the Tai Po fire.
Various bars, including Quinary, The Opposites, Draftland, Penicillin, The Green Door and Tell Camellia, donated all of their gross income from November 29 to ongoing campaigns and trusted charities.

The Poet is extending support by continuing to donate all proceeds of its Lychee Spritz cocktail to the government’s Support Fund for Wang Fuk Court in Tai Po up until this Friday night.
By purchasing the HK$100 Lychee Spritz this Friday, patrons can provide a tangible form of support: these critical funds will go towards rebuilding homes and offering long-term assistance to the families affected by the fire.
13 Staunton Street, Central
2. Firsts: Hong Kong
Whether you are a seasoned bibliophile or a curious newcomer, antique lovers can embark on a journey through history and literature at Firsts: Hong Kong, a rare book fair set to return to the Hong Kong Maritime Museum in Central from December 5 to 7.
This event, presented by the Antiquarian Booksellers’ Association, is free to enter for all.

Visitors can discover and acquire collectors’ highlights such as an original handwritten letter from acclaimed philosopher Soren Kierkegaard, the first Chinese-English dictionary written by an American missionary, and paintings dating back to the 1850s.
British exhibitor Peter Harrington Rare Books will even display a 1500 edition of De le maraveliose cose del Mondo (literally “of the marvellous things of the world”), a travelogue based on Marco Polo’s nearly 20 years in Kublai Khan’s Yuan dynasty court. Polo’s account became one of the most influential European sources on China, Japan, Tibet and Southeast Asia of the time.
11 Man Kwong Street, Central Pier No 8, Hong Kong
3. Slow Living with Pause & Breathe
Escape the rush of city life this Sunday and head to the mindfulness retreat “Slow Living: One-Day Offline Mindfulness Day”.
The event, taking place from 10am to 4pm, aims to help participants reset and reconnect with fellow practitioners from local charity Pause & Breathe in the tranquil environment of the Jao Tsung-I Academy in Lai Chi Kok.

Meditation sessions guided by professional mindfulness leaders will help regulate your body and breath.
Slow living encourages mental well-being and presence over constant rushing, and a tech detox is the essential first step. Participants will be required to turn off or silence their mobile phones during this workshop.
Priced at HK$480 per person, this experience includes a nourishing vegetarian lunch to power you through the day. Limited spots are available. Registration details can be found here.
800 Castle Peak Road, Lai Chi Kok
4. 10 films in which to find solace
In times of collective mourning, we often turn to art not to escape our reality, but to find a safe space to process it. Films can offer catharsis – providing a vessel for our tears, a reflection of our resilience and a quiet companion in our grief.
It is in this spirit that the Post has compiled a list of 10 films – some based on real events, others purely fictional – that illustrate how humanity finds the strength to endure even the darkest of hours.

5. Crossroads Foundation
This weekend, you can buy immediate relief items for those affected by the Tai Po fire and donate essentials. As winter approaches and temperatures dip, displaced families will require necessities to stay warm and nourished.
As the focus shifts to supporting rehousing efforts, local charity Crossroads Foundation has created a list of essential items needed. As of December 2, the current high-priority items include single beds, bunk beds, washing machines, refrigerators, televisions and rice cookers. The non-profit organisation is also requesting single-burner stoves and folding tables.
All donations can be dropped off at the Crossroads warehouse, open from Tuesday to Saturday, 10am to 5pm. For more information on how and what to donate, visit the organisation’s webpage or contact 2984 9309.
Building 32, Crossroads Village, 2 Castle Peak Road, Tuen Mun
Find this useful? Look out for our recommendations every Friday online.
Like what you read? Follow SCMP Lifestyle on Facebook, X and Instagram. You can also sign up for our eNewsletter here.

data.title: Moore Threads stock surges fivefold in Shanghai debut as investors rush for AI chipmaker
data.description:
Moore Threads Technology surged by more than fivefold in Shanghai, as enthusiastic investors vied to get shares of the Beijing-based semiconductor supplier of chips for artificial intelligence (AI), helping to clock one of the most dazzling trading debuts in China’s stock market.
Moore’s shares began trading at 650 yuan amid a declining market, a 468 per cent jump from their initial public offer (IPO) price of 114.28 yuan. China’s benchmark CSI300 Index slipped 0.1 per cent.
The surge in Moore reflects China’s prioritisation of technology self-sufficiency in a critical five-year plan that outlined the nation’s economic and social development goals through 2030. China is now engaged in a tit-for-tat competition with the US in the tech area from AI large-language models to sophisticated chips and robotics.
Moore raised 8 billion yuan (US$1.13 billion) in the second-largest IPO on the mainland this year. Proceeds from the IPO will finance its next-generation projects in AI and graphics chips and supplement working capital. The offering is only smaller than Huadian New Energy Group’s US$2.7 billion IPO in July.
More to follow …

data.title: Myanmar’s criticism of Takaichi signals ‘loyalty’ to China ahead of election
data.description:
Myanmar’s junta has grown so dependent on mainland China for political and economic support that it is highlighting issues such as Taiwan to signal “loyalty” towards Beijing and secure backing for an imminent election that the military hopes will legitimise its rule, according to analysts.
This was on display last month when the military-led regime condemned Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s comments on Taiwan in language closely aligned with Beijing’s talking points, reflecting what analysts said was a significant narrowing of Naypyidaw’s foreign policy options since the 2021 coup.
Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China to be reunited by force if necessary. Most countries, including the US, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state, but Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the self-governed island by force and is committed to supplying it with weapons.
In an interview with Xinhua News Agency on November 22, Zaw Min Tun, spokesman for Myanmar’s National Defence and Security Council and deputy information minister, denounced Takaichi’s remarks and reiterated that Myanmar had “always firmly adhered to the one-China principle”.
He argued the Japanese leader’s position showed “no lessons learned from history” by Tokyo and reflected “neither remorse nor responsibility for the crimes committed by Japan across Asia”.
The Taiwan question was purely mainland China’s internal affair, and Myanmar opposed any external interference, Zaw Min Tun said, according to a statement from the Chinese foreign ministry.

The Chinese embassy in Myanmar welcomed the spokesman’s condemnation of Takaichi’s “erroneous remarks”.
On November 7, Takaichi angered Beijing by stating in parliament that a mainland Chinese military attack on Taiwan could be considered a “survival-threatening situation” for Japan.
Such an attack, she said, would also provide a legal basis for Japan’s military to intervene and use force in self-defence.
Sarah Soh, an associate research fellow with the S. Rajaratnam School of International Studies’ regional security architecture programme, said the junta’s condemnation of Japan was “unsurprising” and “a calculated move”.
“The junta is probably looking to secure China’s help to legitimise the coming election,” she said, adding that China had extended political and economic support to Naypyidaw, which had in turn “proactively aligned itself with China by protecting China’s interests in Myanmar”.
“China’s support is paramount to the regime’s survival. The junta will spare no effort to court China’s favour,” Soh said.
Myanmar is scheduled to hold a general election over several phases starting on December 28, a move widely described by international observers and rights groups as a sham.

The poll is seen as an attempt by the junta to extend and legitimise its control of the country after it seized power in the 2021 coup from the elected government headed by Nobel laureate Aung San Suu Kyi.
The ongoing civil war makes holding elections safely in many regions impossible as resistance groups and various ethnic armed organisations control vast tracts of the country’s territory.
China is Myanmar’s second-largest foreign investor after Singapore, with cumulative investments in energy, defence, mining, construction and other sectors amounting to over US$22 billion as of August.
Sreeparna Banerjee, associate fellow at the Delhi-based Observer Research Foundation think tank’s strategic studies programme, said Myanmar’s stance reflected its deepening reliance on China at a time when the pool of countries willing to engage openly with the junta had “significantly narrowed”.
“By echoing [mainland] China’s sensitivities on Taiwan, the regime signals loyalty to its most dependable backer, underscoring how constrained its foreign-policy autonomy has become,” she noted.
Many in Myanmar have pushed back against the junta’s statement, stressing that it reflects only its position and not the will of the people.
This shows “just how disconnected the junta’s external messaging is from public sentiment”, according to Sreeparna.


Countries that maintain close relations with Myanmar include China, Russia, India and Thailand, with Beijing and Moscow being its most important allies.
Apart from providing political and economic support, China has shielded Myanmar from UN Security Council sanctions targeting the junta.
Ties between Myanmar and Russia have also grown closer since the coup.
Japan’s involvement in Myanmar
Japan has historically been Myanmar’s largest aid donor, but following the coup, Tokyo suspended all new Official Development Assistance projects and loans to the Southeast Asian nation.
Some major Japanese companies, such as beverage firm Kirin, have divested from joint ventures or projects with military links due to ethical concerns and international pressure.
However, trade relations and certain projects have continued, including major infrastructure developments such as the Thilawa special economic zone and the Bago River bridge.
Myanmar was not on the list of Japan’s priorities due to Tokyo’s preoccupation with domestic and foreign policy challenges, Soh said.

Takaichi had to focus on securing her domestic allies’ support, managing economic issues such as inflation and wage stagnation, and curbing rising anti-immigration sentiments, she added.
On foreign policy, Takaichi had to build rapport with the US administration under President Donald Trump and manage the diplomatic fallout with China, she noted.
“Japan will likely look to Asean to influence developments in Myanmar and express its continual support for Asean-led mechanisms and efforts,” Soh said, referring to the 11-member Association of Southeast Asian Nations.
While Japan’s leverage in Myanmar had diminished since the coup, it remained one of the few actors with access to both the junta and elements of the opposition, Sreeparna said.
For instance, Tokyo could use its aid and diplomatic channels to press for limited concessions from the junta and expand humanitarian access in Myanmar, according to Sreeparna.
“Its influence today is more incremental than transformative.”

data.title: China’s unmanned Moyujian strike helicopter is ready for plateau combat tests
data.description:
China’s unmanned reconnaissance and strike helicopter tailored for deployment in plateau areas is ready for critical combat testing.
The new multipurpose medium-sized helicopter was displayed on November 27 at an air show in Zhuhai, in southern China, where the next stage of testing was announced by its developer.
The Moyujian was exhibited at the second Asia General Aviation Exhibition (Aero Asia), held from November 27 to 30.
Aero Asia, jointly organised by the German General Aviation Exhibition and Airshow China, was held for the first time in 2023. The event, which takes place every two years, is China’s only general aviation exhibition held with a foreign partner, and the most recent edition featured 174 Chinese and foreign aircraft.


The unmanned helicopter was shown in its camouflage livery on a static display. The display also featured a video of the aircraft’s first successful live-fire target test at high altitude, which its developers said marked progress in its reliability for plateau operations.
Developed by the Chinese private company Sichuan Tengden Technology, the helicopter made its maiden flight in 2019.
The company said the uncrewed vehicle had excellent deployment capabilities on plateaus and at sea, and could be applied to reconnaissance and strike operations, emergency rescue, communications relay and forest fire prevention.
An unmanned helicopter operator from Tengden told state broadcaster CCTV that the Moyujian completed live-fire target tests in July at an altitude exceeding 4,000 metres (13,100 feet) while carrying two missiles to conduct strike tests on both moving and stationary targets.
“The next phase of testing will be a target test mission using satellite communication and beyond visual line of sight control, at an altitude of over 6,000 metres,” the operator added.
According to Tengden’s official website, the Moyujian has a maximum range and flight time of 1,000km (621 miles) and nine hours, respectively. It says the aircraft features a high-altitude take-off and landing capability with a service ceiling of 7,200 metres, specifically tailored for extremely harsh environments.
In the six years since its maiden flight, the Moyujian has completed six years of tests in complex high-altitude terrain, payload and mission systems, marine flight applications and forest fire prevention, and has entered the combat testing phase, according to the company.
The aircraft’s name comes from Zhang Qing, a character in the ancient Chinese novel Water Margin nicknamed Moyujian, or “featherless arrow”, who could kill enemies by throwing stones.
Its action and attack capability at high altitudes and in harsh weather is crucial for operations on China’s highest plateau, the Tibetan Plateau. The area includes the rugged Himalayan border with India, where the altitude typically ranges from 4,000 to 5,000 metres.
Its range and wind resistance also make the helicopter suitable for maritime operations, including wide-area patrols over the ocean, such as in the South China Sea.
Beijing has doubled down on developing unmanned equipment, joining the global race to create drones that can be used for civilian and military purposes. The drive has resulted in landmark achievements for some all-terrain and all-weather equipment.
China showcased new uncrewed equipment at its Victory Day parade on September 3, including submarines, loyal wingmen, drone combat vehicles and robot dogs.
Its Chinook-style heavy-lift unmanned helicopter Boying T1400, which is capable of operating in extreme conditions from the Himalayas to the South China Sea, completed its maiden flight last month.


Beijing also deployed drones to a military airport in the Shigatse region of Tibet in August and September, according to satellite images.
And in October, it set up a high plateau test centre for unmanned equipment in Ali, a prefecture in Tibet also known as Ngari, according to state news agency Xinhua.
The Xinjiang Military Command in January conducted a logistics support exercise at an altitude of 5,300 metres on a plateau in the Karakoram Range, featuring robot dogs and unmanned combat vehicles, another Xinhua report said.

data.title: Are Singaporean Chinese the ‘least attractive’? Viral image ignites beauty debate
data.description:
A widely shared image on social media depicting colour sketches of men from different regions in China has ignited debate on the physical appearance of Singaporean Chinese based on their ancestry, with online users concluding they are the least attractive of the gene pool.
But psychotherapists say perceptions of beauty might be warped by media and celebrity culture, warning against the normalisation of conversations centred on physical appearance.
The discussion was prompted by drawings of male faces from China’s northwest, northeast, southwest, southeast and Xinjiang regions, which went viral on social media this week and racked up millions of views.
The original source of the image and its artist are still unknown.
In the depictions, the male face from southeastern China is shown to have a rounder nose, protruding jaw and a face less angular than his counterparts.
Some online users – many of whom claimed to be Singaporean – said the artist’s impression was representative of the unattractiveness of ethnic Chinese people in the city state, noting how the archetype paled in comparison with others of the same ethnicity in different regions.
More than two-thirds of Chinese in the Asian financial hub belong to Hokkien, Teochew and Cantonese dialect groups, according to the 2020 Singapore census data. The roots of these dialect groups are traced back to the southeastern provinces of China, such as Fujian and Guangdong.
Chinese make up 74 per cent of Singapore’s 6.11 million population, while Malays and Indians form 13.6 per cent and 9 per cent, respectively.
“Singaporeans are lacking in many ways in beauty, including maintenance and aesthetic improvements,” a user said. “Somehow, Malaysians know how to doll themselves up better.”

“Being a Singaporean myself, I don’t find any Vivian Chow or Edison Chen here,” wrote another user, referring to the popular singers, noting that the warm climate might be a huge disincentive for individuals to dress up.
“What is less attractive about us, I believe, is that we are very petite, short and have darker skin,” a third said. “We are much shorter than our northern counterparts.”
Some users argued that the lack of physical beauty was due to an absence of grooming culture in Singapore and others stressed that beauty standards were subjective.
Singaporeans were less likely to feel pressure to dress up or enhance their appearance with cosmetic procedures compared with other countries with a celebrity culture that perpetuated high beauty standards, according to one user.
In response to a comment that attributed Singaporeans’ unattractiveness to a smaller stature, a commenter said: “It depends on personal preferences, some actually prefer petite frames. Beauty is in the eye of the beholder.”
The collage was also criticised for being unrepresentative and false, with many saying it spurred racism within and towards the Chinese community.
“We are all Chinese. We all have a variety of facial features and no features are better than others; we should be proud of them,” one user said.
Norman Li, a psychology professor at Singapore Management University (SMU), said individuals might have a warped perception of those from other countries due to the media.
“Sometimes we find people from other places better looking because we’re looking at movie stars and celebrities who have been cherry picked for their good looks,” he said.

Singaporean women might be more critical in perceiving physical attractiveness due to their financial independence, which leads them to hyperfocusing on looks, Li added.
While appearances may differ across regions in China due to the variations in climate and resources, the caricatures do not warrant sweeping generalisations, he said.
“An exaggerated picture isn’t going to answer the question,” he said. “From a scientific standpoint, you need to collect a random sample of people from different regions and conduct a study with objective ratings.”
Jared Ng, a psychiatrist at Connections MindHealth, warned that constant online conversation tying nationality, race and diaspora identity also risks reinforcing insecurities, prejudice and social hierarchies.
Shilpa Madan, an assistant professor of marketing at SMU, said the image normalised treating appearance as a legitimate basis for ranking whole communities, fuelling prejudice against “less attractive” groups.
Such debates could encourage narrow beauty standards influenced by popular culture and foster a culture of comparison and search for validation, according to experts.

Madan said: “It suggests that only certain regional ‘types’ of faces are desirable while others are inherently less attractive, encouraging people to see their own faces as less than relative to an ideal they can fully reach.”
Asians already experienced high levels of pressure to conform to such ideals due to culture and were more likely to undergo extreme and risky surgery and enhancement to change their appearance, she said.
“Today, many societies prize a slimmer build, sharper features and specific facial proportions, shaped heavily by global media and online comparison,” Ng said.
To underscore ever-changing beauty ideals, Ng cited the example of Yang Guifei, one of the “Four Beauties” of ancient China, who was admired for her fuller figure.
“When standards can shift so quickly across eras and cultures, it becomes clear that attractiveness is not a fixed truth, but a story shaped by society, economy and taste,” he said.
“Seeing it this way helps us treat ourselves and others with more gentleness and allows the conversation to move away from judgment towards understanding.”

data.title: Meta plans budget cuts as high as 30% to its metaverse efforts in 2026
data.description:
Meta Platforms CEO Mark Zuckerberg is expected to cut resources for building the so-called metaverse, an effort that he once framed as the future of the company and the reason for changing its name from Facebook.
Executives were considering potential budget cuts as high as 30 per cent for the metaverse group next year, which includes the virtual worlds product Meta Horizon Worlds and its Quest virtual reality (VR) unit, according to people familiar with the talks, who asked not to be named while discussing private company plans.
Cuts that high were most likely to include lay-offs as early as January, according to the people, though a final decision had not yet been made.
Meta confirmed a reduction in resources for the metaverse and said savings were expected to funnel towards other futuristic projects within Meta’s Reality Labs division, including artificial intelligence glasses and other wearables.
“Within our overall Reality Labs portfolio we are shifting some of our investment from metaverse towards AI glasses and wearables given the momentum there. We aren’t planning any broader changes than that,” the spokesperson said in a statement.

The proposed metaverse cuts were part of the company’s annual budget planning for 2026, which included a series of meetings at Zuckerberg’s compound in Hawaii last month, people familiar with the company said.
Zuckerberg had asked Meta executives to look for 10 per cent cuts across the board, which had been the standard request during similar budget cycles the past few years, they added.
The metaverse group was asked to cut deeper this year given that Meta has not seen the level of industry-wide competition over the technology that it once expected, they said.
Most of the proposed cuts were likely to hit Meta’s VR group, which made up the bulk of metaverse-related spend, the people said. Cuts would also target Horizon Worlds.
The entire metaverse effort has drawn scrutiny from investors, who have seen it as a drain on resources, as well as from watchdogs, who have alleged that children’s privacy and safety have been compromised in the virtual worlds.
Meta’s vision for the metaverse has not taken off despite Zuckerberg’s conviction, which he still has, that people will one day work and play in virtual worlds. In 2021, as Facebook was facing fallout for user safety and privacy issues, Zuckerberg rebranded the company around the idea of the metaverse and started spending heavily on the vision.

The metaverse group sits within Reality Labs, the Meta division focused on long-term bets like VR headsets and augmented reality glasses. That group has lost more than US$70 billion since the start of 2021.
Zuckerberg has largely stopped mentioning the metaverse in public and on company earnings calls, and is instead focused on developing the large AI models that underpin AI chatbots and other generative AI products, as well as the hardware products that are more linked to those experiences, like Meta’s Ray-Ban smart display glasses.
Some analysts and investors have long advocated that Zuckerberg rid himself of Reality Labs products that continue to drain resources without providing much revenue in return.
In April, Mike Proulx, a vice-president at research and advisory firm Forrester, predicted that Meta would “shutter its metaverse projects, like Horizon Worlds” before the end of the year.
Meta’s “Reality Labs division continues to be a leaky bucket”, he said in an email at the time, pointing to the unit’s losses. Shutting down metaverse efforts, Proulx said, “would allow the company to give more focus to its AI projects including Llama, Meta AI and AI glasses”.
Meta is still committed to building consumer hardware, and recently hired Apple’s top design executive to help.

data.title: Hong Kong stocks head for weekly loss before key rate-decision meetings in US, Japan
data.description:
Hong Kong stocks fell on Friday, set to record a loss for a week in which the market traded in a rangebound fashion before key decisions by the world’s central banks.
The Hang Seng Index dropped 0.4 per cent to 25,837.80 as of 10.03am local time. The Hang Seng Tech Index dropped 0.5 per cent.
On the mainland, the CSI 300 Index climbed 0.1 per cent and the Shanghai Composite Index was little changed.
Trip.com Group tumbled 2.1 per cent to HK$544.50 and NetEase lost 1.8 per cent to HK$213.40. Alibaba Group Holding slid 0.8 per cent to HK$153.20 and Tencent Holdings slipped 0.8 per cent to HK$607.
Caution prevailed before the rate decision meetings by the Federal Reserve and the Bank of Japan in the coming week. While a rate cut in the US will boost risk appetite, a hike in Japan will increase the risk of unwinding the yen carry trade, causing turbulence across global financial markets.
China’s annual central economic work conference, expected to take place this month, is also in focus, with traders paying close attention to how Beijing will revive growth.
Three companies started trading. In Hong Kong, Guangdong Tianyu Semiconductor slumped 23 per cent to HK$44.44 and Guangzhou Xiao Noodles Catering Management tumbled 25 per cent to HK$5.25. In Shanghai, artificial-intelligence chipmaker Moore Threads Technology surged 420 per cent to 595 yuan.
Other major Asian markets were mixed. Japan’s Nikkei 225 slid 1.5 per cent, while South Korea’s Kospi rose 0.6 per cent and Australia’s S&P/ASX 200 added 0.1 per cent.

data.title: Style Edit: Hongkong Land’s Noëlia brings a wintry festive scene to the Landmark Atrium
data.description:
This festive season, Landmark Atrium has been transformed into “Noëlia”, a dreamy French chateau-inspired winter village and the luxury mall’s largest-ever holiday installation. Soaring 11 metres high and spanning 30 metres across, the sprawling fixture also hosts Hong Kong’s biggest (and probably only) interactive wishing lake, Lake Lumière.

“For generations, Landmark has been synonymous with Central’s most festive moments,” says Alexander Li, director and head of retail for Hong Kong & Macau at Hongkong Land. “This year, with Noëlia at Landmark and our partnership with the Hong Kong Tourism Board, we not only honour that legacy but reaffirm our commitment to keeping Central vibrant and inspiring. It reflects the spirit of our ongoing Tomorrow’s Central transformation – delivering unique new experiences and making Central more exciting than ever.”

Running through New Year’s Day, the experience promises maximum immersion, inviting guests to slide down an eight-metre Starlit Slide or create keepsakes at the photo booth by Don’t Lxxk Up, a cult brand from Korea. While wandering the wintry village, visitors can stop at stores such as Livres & Lumières, a cosy bookshop with a smoking chimney; and Floured & Frosted, a cake shop with mouthwatering assortments.

Adding a touch of realism to the storybook setting, Landmark has partnered with luxury brands Baccarat and Christofle so that Theo’s Studio is lit by a Baccarat chandelier, while Christofle’s Silver Reverie displays elegant French silverwork.

For those who want to take a piece of Noëlia back home, a curated range of suitably festive merchandise is available at The Gift Shop.
Entry to the village is ticketed, with proceeds supporting local charity SideBySide, cementing the village’s message of joy and giving.

Beyond Landmark, Hongkong Land’s partnership with the Hong Kong Tourism Board further transforms the area into a dazzling “Winter Wonderland in Central”, highlighted by Starlight Boulevard’s enchanting illuminations along Chater Road – a revival of a beloved city tradition from past decades.
